policy_statement: Convert 50% of non-recycled garbage to energy by 2030.
mechanism_description: Converting residual non-recycled waste into energy diverts it from landfills, avoiding methane emissions while recovering usable energy that can offset fossil generation.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Residual waste and wastewater biosolids are converted into usable electricity/heat, increasing energy recovered per unit waste and reducing demand for virgin energy inputs for the same delivered service.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste management system change → ↓ uncontrolled landfill anaerobic decomposition (↓ CH₄) + controlled energy recovery → ↓ net GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Municipal systems shift residual solid waste and/or wastewater sludge from landfill disposal toward waste-to-energy pathways (incineration with energy recovery, anaerobic digestion/biogas capture, wastewater cogeneration).  
(2) Process: Diverting waste from landfills reduces anaerobic decomposition that would emit methane; instead, carbon is oxidized in controlled conditions and/or methane is captured and combusted to CO₂ while producing energy.  
(3) Outcome: Lower lifecycle climate impact via avoided landfill CH₄ emissions and additional reductions when recovered electricity/heat displaces higher-emitting grid or fossil thermal energy.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced net GHG emissions (especially avoided landfill methane) from diverting organic/residual waste to controlled energy recovery.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Waste is redirected from landfill (high CH₄ leakage risk) to controlled conversion with energy recovery, producing a net reduction in GHGs—this is a direct mitigation pathway.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: While energy recovery and “using waste as a resource” is important, the largest climate effect across instances is typically avoided methane (high GWP) plus displacement of fossil energy, both of which are mitigation outcomes rather than adaptation.  
3) Inherent secondary chain(s):  
   - Resource Efficiency is inherent because the mechanism necessarily recovers usable energy from a previously discarded stream, reducing required external energy inputs for equivalent energy services.  
4) Edge cases applied: If the waste-to-energy pathway is mass-burn incineration of fossil-plastic-heavy waste without meaningful landfill methane avoidance (or if landfill gas capture already near-complete), the net mitigation benefit can be smaller or ambiguous; however, the mechanism as defined includes landfill methane avoidance and energy recovery, keeping mitigation dominant.  
5) Labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy implementing this mechanism aims to reduce net emissions through landfill diversion/CH₄ avoidance and energy recovery, so Mitigation (with inherent Resource Efficiency) fits across contexts.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net mitigation depends on waste composition (biogenic vs plastic), baseline landfill methane capture, and whether recovered energy actually displaces fossil generation; some implementations can increase local air pollutants, but that does not change the primary climate category.
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: reduced landfill volume/land-use demand; local air quality impacts may improve or worsen depending on WtE technology and controls; energy security via local generation.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Convert to energy” could imply different pathways (incineration, anaerobic digestion/biogas, RDF) with different emissions profiles; the text is a target without specifying the implementing instrument (e.g., mandate vs incentive), so it is classified as target-setting.
